Arguments.prompt: Download the Oregon state boundary shapefile from the US Census Bureau TIGER/GENZ2022 dataset at https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/GENZ2022/shp/cb_2022_us_state_20m.zip. Also download the county boundaries shapefile from https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/GENZ2022/shp/cb_2022_us_county_5m.zip. From the county layer, filter to only those records where the state FIPS code equals '41' (Oregon counties). Reproject both the Oregon state boundary layer and the Oregon county boundary layer to EPSG:5070 (Albers Equal Area Conic). From the Oregon state boundary geometry, reproject it to EPSG:4326 and extract the bounding box as (min_longitude, min_latitude, max_longitude, max_latitude) expressed in EPSG:4326 (WGS84 geographic coordinates). Write the following outputs to the local staging directory so FaaSr can upload them to S3: (1) the Oregon state boundary as a GeoJSON file named 'oregon_state_boundary.geojson' in EPSG:5070, (2) the Oregon county boundaries as a GeoJSON file named 'oregon_county_boundaries.geojson' in EPSG:5070, and (3) the Oregon bounding box in EPSG:4326 as a JSON file named 'oregon_bbox.json' containing a dictionary with keys 'min_lon', 'min_lat', 'max_lon', 'max_lat'.

Using the Oregon bounding box JSON file produced by the previous step (containing keys 'min_lon', 'min_lat', 'max_lon', 'max_lat' in EPSG:4326), query the Microsoft Planetary Computer STAC API for the collection 'modis-13A1-061' (MODIS 16-day NDVI composite at 500-meter resolution). Process a single calendar year of data: use the year 2023, querying month by month from January 2023 through December 2023 (12 months total). This fixed one-year window ensures the downstream 4-column by 3-row monthly map grid (12 panels) is fully populated and avoids memory or timeout issues from multi-year queries. For each month, construct a datetime interval string spanning the first to last day of that month and use the Oregon bounding box as the spatial filter (bbox parameter). Use the pystac_client library to connect to the Planetary Computer STAC API at 'https://planetarycomputer.microsoft.com/api/stac/v1'. If a given month returns zero items from the STAC API, skip that month silently without logging or raising any error. For each month that returns at least one item, collect the list of STAC item dictionaries (serialized via item.to_dict()) along with the month label (formatted as 'YYYY-MM'). Save the complete collection of monthly STAC query results as a JSON file named 'modis_stac_results.json', structured as a list of objects each containing keys 'month' (string 'YYYY-MM') and 'items' (list of STAC item dictionaries). Only include months that returned at least one result. Write this file to the local staging directory so FaaSr can upload it to S3.

Using the MODIS STAC results JSON file (containing a list of monthly entries each with a 'month' string and 'items' list of STAC item dictionaries) and the Oregon state boundary GeoJSON file (in EPSG:5070) produced by previous steps, process each month's satellite imagery to produce a monthly NDVI composite raster. For each monthly entry in the STAC results: (1) Reconstruct each STAC item from its dictionary using pystac.Item.from_dict(). (2) Sign each item's asset URLs using planetary_computer.sign(item) before accessing them. (3) Access the asset named '500m_16_days_NDVI' from each signed item. (4) Load each tile using rioxarray (rxr.open_rasterio) with the signed href. (5) Reproject each tile individually to EPSG:5070 using rio.reproject(). (6) After reprojecting all tiles for the month, mosaic them together into a single raster using rioxarray.merge.merge_arrays(). (7) Clip the mosaicked raster to the Oregon state boundary polygon using rio.clip() with the GeoJSON geometries, ensuring the CRS matches. (8) Convert raw integer NDVI values to floating-point NDVI by dividing by 10000.0, casting to float32. (9) Mask out any pixel values outside the physically valid NDVI range of -1.0 to 1.0 by setting them to NaN. (10) If multiple 16-day composite scenes (tiles) exist within the same calendar month entry — that is, multiple items listed under the same 'month' key in the STAC results — compute a pixel-wise nanmean across all those valid (unmasked) scenes from that single month entry to produce one monthly composite array. This nanmean is computed only within a single month's scenes, not across different months. If any month encounters an error during processing after the STAC query succeeded, log the error message and month label and continue to the next month without raising an exception. Collect all successfully processed monthly composites. Save the result as a single multi-band GeoTIFF file named 'ndvi_monthly_composites.tif' where each band corresponds to one month in chronological order, and save a companion JSON file named 'ndvi_month_labels.json' containing a list of month label strings (format 'YYYY-MM') in the same order as the bands in the GeoTIFF. Write both files to the local staging directory so FaaSr can upload them to S3.

Using the multi-band monthly NDVI composites GeoTIFF file and the companion month labels JSON file produced by the previous step, compute the mean NDVI time series for Oregon. For each band (month) in the GeoTIFF, load the band as a 2D array and compute the nanmean across all spatially valid (non-NaN) pixels to produce a single scalar mean NDVI value for that month. Pair each scalar mean with its corresponding month label string from the JSON file. Save the resulting time series as a CSV file named 'ndvi_timeseries.csv' with two columns: 'month' (string in 'YYYY-MM' format) and 'mean_ndvi' (float, rounded to six decimal places). The CSV must have a header row and one data row per month. Write this file to the local staging directory so FaaSr can upload it to S3.

Using the multi-band monthly NDVI composites GeoTIFF file, the month labels JSON file, the Oregon state boundary GeoJSON file (in EPSG:5070), and the Oregon county boundaries GeoJSON file (in EPSG:5070) produced by previous steps, generate a map grid visualization of monthly NDVI composites. Create a matplotlib figure at a 16:9 aspect ratio with a 4-column by 3-row grid of subplots (12 panels total). Display months in chronological order, one panel per month. If there are fewer than 12 months of processed data, leave remaining panels blank with no content and no visible axes. For each panel: (1) Display the monthly NDVI composite raster using imshow or a raster plotting method with the 'YlGn' colormap. (2) Overlay the Oregon state boundary as a polygon outline with no fill (facecolor='none') and a dark gray edge color (edgecolor='#333333') with linewidth of 1.5. (3) Overlay the Oregon county boundaries as polygon outlines with no fill (facecolor='none') and a medium gray edge color (edgecolor='#888888') with linewidth of 0.5. (4) Label the panel with the abbreviated month name and year derived from the 'YYYY-MM' month label string (for example, '2023-01' should display as 'Jan 2023'), placed as a title or text annotation in the upper-left corner of the panel. (5) Remove all axis ticks and axis spines from the panel for a clean cartographic appearance. Compute the 2nd and 98th percentiles of all valid NDVI pixel values across all displayed monthly composites combined, and use these as the shared vmin and vmax for the colormap across all panels. Add a single shared colorbar below or beside the grid labeled 'NDVI'. Save the figure as a PNG file named 'ndvi_monthly_maps.png' at 200 dots per inch. Write this file to the local staging directory so FaaSr can upload it to S3.

Using the NDVI time series CSV file produced by a previous step (with columns 'month' in 'YYYY-MM' format and 'mean_ndvi' as float), generate a line plot of the mean statewide NDVI over time. Parse the 'month' column as datetime objects (first day of each month). Plot mean_ndvi on the y-axis versus time on the x-axis as a continuous line with circular markers. Label the x-axis 'Month' and the y-axis 'Mean NDVI'. Add a light background grid (alpha 0.3) to aid readability. Identify all interior local maxima and minima in the mean_ndvi series — that is, indices i (where 0 < i < len-1) where mean_ndvi[i] > mean_ndvi[i-1] and mean_ndvi[i] > mean_ndvi[i+1] (local max) or mean_ndvi[i] < mean_ndvi[i-1] and mean_ndvi[i] < mean_ndvi[i+1] (local min). Do not annotate the first or last data points even if they appear to be extrema. For each identified interior local maximum and minimum, add a text annotation showing the abbreviated month name (e.g., 'Jan') and the mean_ndvi value rounded to three decimal places, offset slightly above the point for maxima and below for minima to avoid overlap with the line. Set the figure title to 'Oregon Mean NDVI Time Series'. Save the figure as a PNG file named 'ndvi_timeseries_plot.png' at 150 dots per inch. Write this file to the local staging directory so FaaSr can upload it to S3.
